# 

# **A. Title of the Study** {#a.-title-of-the-study}

Decentralized ZKP Authorization Meshes for Industrial Robotics in DIL Environments

# **B. Statement of the Problem** {#b.-statement-of-the-problem}

## **B.1 Problem Definition** {#b.1-problem-definition}

Modern automated logistics and Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) operating within Department of Defense (DoD) Contested Logistics environments face catastrophic drops in Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) \[3, 8\] when cloud-dependent authentication fails under high-density signal jamming. This research investigates the exact network-failure threshold (p\*) where decentralized Edge-First Mobile Ad hoc Network (MANET) authentication \[6\] utilizing Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs) maintains operational continuity and bounded mechanical latency on resource-constrained nodes, compared to centralized OAuth 2.0 architectures. Specifically, the study quantifies the algorithmic latency tax of ZKP selective disclosure and applies M/M/1 queueing theory to extrapolate MANET saturation density in localized autonomous clusters.

## **B.2 Scope and Limitations** {#b.2-scope-and-limitations}

This study strictly scopes its application domain to DoD Contested Logistics, utilizing industrial manufacturing as a secondary illustrative case. 

* Hardware Scope: The physical testbed is constrained to a 10-node cluster. Edge compute nodes consist of Arduino Nano 33 BLEs (ARM Cortex-M4), representing resource-constrained endpoints. The robotic hardware is isolated to the Niryo Ned2 6-axis manipulator, utilizing MoveIt library integration for trajectory execution.  
* Threat Model: The threat model evaluates Byzantine Insiders (compromised legitimate nodes) \[1, 5\] and Side-Channel Attackers. Defense relies on a dynamically updated trust score (Γ) and constant-time implementations with scalar blinding to mitigate timing and Simple Power Analysis (SPA) attacks.  
* Measurements: Network disruptions are induced via randomized port-blocking. Physical consequences are measured via G-code execution disruption and emergency-stop deceleration latency, captured physically via a wrist-mounted 9-axis IMU. This latency must not exceed a strict operational ceiling of 500 milliseconds, defined as the fail-safe boundary before involuntary mechanical shutdown occurs under ISO 13849-1 safety standards.

**B.3 Research Hypotheses**  
Hypothesis 1 (Crossover Threshold):

* H₁,₀ (Null): The Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) of centralized OAuth 2.0 and Edge-First MANET authentication architectures do not significantly differ, regardless of the network packet-loss rate (⍴).  
* H₁,a (Alternative): There exists a packet-loss threshold p\* below which centralized OAuth 2.0 has a lower MTTR than Edge-First MANET, and above which the ordering reverses.

Hypothesis 2 (Cost of Selective Disclosure):

* H₂,₀ (Null): Schnorr ZKP authentication with selective disclosure incurs no statistically significant latency variance compared to standard ECDSA verification on Cortex-M4 hardware.  
* H₂,a (Alternative): Schnorr ZKP authentication with attribute selective disclosure \[4, 9\] incurs a quantifiable, higher verification latency per byte of disclosed attribute than ECDSA, representing the necessary computational tax for enabling non-disclosure privacy properties.

---

Hypothesis 3 (Queue Saturation):

* H₃,₀ (Null): The MTTR in the MANET cluster scales linearly with the number of nodes (*n*) and is independent of M/M/1 queueing parameters.  
* H₃,a (Alternative): Under a gossip protocol with verification rate \[2, 7\]  per node and message-arrival rate  scaling as O(n²), MTTR follows the M/M/1 queue formula MTTR(n)= 1 \- (n) and diverges asymptotically as *n* approaches the saturation density n\*=c , where *c* is the per-node gossip overhead constant.

**B.4 Major Assumptions**

1. Trust Initialization & Arbitration: The EWMA smoothing parameter is strictly pre-registered at *ɑ \= 0.1*, with a reward of \+0.1 per successful verification, a penalty of \-0.5 per failed verification, and a fail-safe node exclusion threshold of 0.3.  
2. Physical Hardware Boundaries: Nodes operate within standard thermal and power specifications. The kinetic destruction of hardware nodes by adversary action is outside the scope of this network resiliency model.  
3. Scalability: Because physical hardware is limited, large-scale behavior (*N=100*) is mathematically extrapolated using NS-3 network simulations calibrated against baseline service rates empirically captured on the 10-node testbed.

# **C. Justification for and Significance of the Study** {#c.-justification-for-and-significance-of-the-study}

## **C.1 Reason for Problem Selection** {#c.1-reason-for-problem-selection}

The transition from centralized to decentralized authentication in automated logistics is currently treated as a binary architectural choice rather than a dynamic operational requirement. In Department of Defense Contested Logistics, environments are not static; network degradation occurs rapidly under targeted Electronic Warfare. Currently, autonomous system designers lack the mathematical parameters required to build dynamic fail-safe logic. Isolating the precise packet-loss crossover point (p\*) provides the exact telemetry threshold required for an autonomous robotic cluster to sever its reliance on a jammed centralized server and initiate an Edge-First MANET, minimizing mechanical downtime.

**C.2 Relation to Principal Literature**

Current literature presents a bifurcated landscape that fails to adequately address bare-metal industrial robotics in contested environments. Research in cryptographic privacy proposes Zero-Knowledge Proofs for IoT data sharing but fundamentally treats cryptographic execution as a zero-cost event. These theoretical models fail to operationalize the latency cost per byte of selective disclosure, ignoring the strict processing ceilings of edge hardware like the ARM Cortex-M4. Conversely, literature focusing on MANET saturation relies heavily on arbitrary curve-fitting \[2, 7\] to describe localized network congestion. This descriptive approach fails in realistic scenarios because it cannot be reliably extrapolated. This study bridges both gaps by empirically quantifying the ZKP security tax on bare-metal hardware and replacing arbitrary curve-fitting with predictive M/M/1 queueing theory to mathematically determine network saturation.

**C.3 Importance and Significance**

## This study is significant because it provides a deterministic, mathematically grounded framework for scaling decentralized trust in Disconnected, Intermittent, and Limited (DIL) environments. By quantifying the exact computational tax of selective disclosure and the network threshold for optimal MANET deployment, this research equips systems engineers with the critical parameters necessary to design autonomous clusters that can survive and operate through high-density signal jamming without catastrophic losses in logistical throughput.

# **D. Methodology** {#d.-methodology}

## **D.1 Research Design** {#d.1-research-design}

This study employs a mixed-methods quantitative experimental design, partitioned into an empirical physical phase and a deterministic simulation phase. The physical phase utilizes a 10-node hardware testbed to capture foundational cryptographic and network service rates under synthetically degraded conditions. The simulation phase utilizes the NS-3 discrete-event network simulator to mathematically extrapolate the empirical data, specifically evaluating Mobile Ad-hoc Network (MANET) saturation limits (*N=100*) utilizing M/M/1 queueing theory.

## **D.2 Study Population and Setting** {#d.2-study-population-and-setting}

The physical study population consists of a localized autonomous robotic cluster representing a Department of Defense Contested Logistics node. The cluster is comprised of one Niryo Ned2 6-axis manipulator, one Cortex-A supervisor node (Raspberry Pi 4), and nine Cortex-M4 edge compute nodes (Arduino Nano 33 BLE). The setting is a controlled laboratory environment where network topology is artificially partitioned and degraded to simulate high-density Electronic Warfare (EW) signal jamming.

## **D.3 Data Collection Schedule** {#d.3-data-collection-schedule}

Data collection will occur in three sequential phases over a twelve-week period. Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4) involves baseline hardware instrumentation, the isolation of cryptographic latency metrics, and the execution of a 60-trial pilot study to compute statistical variance. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-8) executes the physical packet-loss sweep (0-30%) to identify the crossover threshold (p\*) and measure the robotic emergency-stop deceleration latency, including partition-rejoin reconciliation testing. Phase 3 (Weeks 9-12) involves the parameterization and execution of the NS-3 network simulations to evaluate large-scale cluster saturation.

## **D.4 Data Collection Instruments** {#d.4-data-collection-instruments}

Data collection relies on deterministic hardware and software instrumentation rather than arbitrary network logging:

1. Cryptographic Latency: Execution latency on the Cortex-M4 edge nodes is measured by directly querying the DWT\_CYCCNT hardware register. Operating at 64 MHz, this provides deterministic 15.625-nanosecond precision, fully isolating algorithmic execution from OS-level timing jitter.  
2. Mechanical Disruption: The physical consequence of network latency on the Niryo Ned2 is captured via a wrist-mounted 9-axis Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). This sensor records acceleration and orientation data to calculate emergency-stop deceleration curves against ISO 13849-1 compliance standards.  
3. Network Disruption and Rejoin: Physical network packet loss and transient partitions are induced and recorded via Scapy ARP cache poisoning scripts and iptables port-blocking at the transport layer. This infrastructure additionally permits the preliminary observation of partition-rejoin reconciliation phases, providing baseline telemetry on trust score synchronization when isolated nodes re-enter the mesh.  
4. Baseline Arrival Rate (): To empirically capture the message-arrival rate for M/M/1 queueing calibration, transport-layer hooking will be utilized. A libpcap wrapper or tshark instance running on the Raspberry Pi 4 supervisor node will passively sniff incoming UDP packets on the CycloneDDS multicast port during an undisturbed baseline, capturing absolute network truth prior to NS-3 ingestion.

## **D.5 Key Terms and Concepts** {#d.5-key-terms-and-concepts}

* p\* (Crossover Threshold): The specific network packet-loss percentage where the Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) of centralized authentication exceeds that of decentralized Edge-First authentication.  
* ZKP Security Tax: The algorithmic latency, measured in CPU clock cycles, required to execute Schnorr Zero-Knowledge Proofs with attribute selective disclosure, relative to standard ECDSA verification.  
* Service Rate (): The deterministic rate at which a node can verify an authentication proof, acting as the fundamental constraint for the M/M/1 queueing model.

## 

## **D.6 Data Processing and Analysis Procedures** {#d.6-data-processing-and-analysis-procedures}

Data processing bridges empirical measurement and queueing theory extrapolation. Prior to the primary statistical evaluation, data from the 60-trial pilot study will be utilized to conduct a statistical power analysis, computing the required sample size to ensure a minimum statistical power of 0.80. For Hypothesis 1, finding the crossover threshold (p\*), a Two-Way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) will evaluate the statistical significance of MTTR variances across differing authentication architectures and packet-loss rates, following Shapiro-Wilk testing for normality. For Hypothesis 3, the NS-3 simulation will be strictly calibrated using physical data. The Cortex-A empirical service rate (supervisor) will dictate the NS-3 application delay. To prevent theoretical livelock, the physical ROS2 CycloneDDS FIFO queue constraints will be mathematically mapped to the NS-3 DropTailQueue limits, and the simulated channel degradation will be driven by an NS-3 BurstErrorModel parameterized by the empirical packet-drop distribution recorded during physical testing.

# **E. Resources Required** {#e.-resources-required}

## **E.1 Hardware Resources** {#e.1-hardware-resources}

The execution of this study requires a localized autonomous robotic cluster configured as a 10-node Mobile Ad-hoc Network (MANET) testbed.

* Supervisor Node: One Raspberry Pi 4 (Cortex-A architecture, 8GB RAM) acting as the authentication arbiter and cluster supervisor.  
* Edge Nodes: Nine Arduino Nano 33 BLE devices (ARM Cortex-M4 architecture) acting as severely resource-constrained sensors and worker nodes.  
* Robotic Hardware: One Niryo Ned2 6-axis collaborative robot to physically execute trajectories and measure mechanical deceleration latency.  
* Network Infrastructure: Localized IEEE 802.11 ad-hoc wireless routing hardware, supplemented by a dedicated wired backend for out-of-band telemetry collection and synthetic packet-loss injection.

## **E.2 Software Resources** {#e.2-software-resources}

* Operating System and Middleware: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS natively hosting ROS2 Humble Hawksbill, utilizing Eclipse CycloneDDS to govern internal node communications and queueing parameters.  
* Simulation and Network: NS-3 discrete-event network simulator for *N=100* scalability extrapolation. Linux Traffic Control (tc) and iptables for transport-layer network degradation.  
* Cryptographic Libraries: Implementation of Schnorr Zero-Knowledge Proofs and ECDSA utilizing custom constant-time wrappers to interface with the hardware cycle counters.

## **E.3 Accessibility and Competency Justification** {#e.3-accessibility-and-competency-justification}

All required hardware is currently procured and operational within the isolated environment of the University of Rhode Island Robotics Laboratory, under the supervision of Dr. Manbir Sodhi. Methodological feasibility is guaranteed by the candidate's demonstrated prior execution of identical or highly similar technical architectures. Specifically, the candidate's prior systems engineering research involving ROS2 SWARM-SLAM architectures, direct experience with Cortex-M4 data collection protocols, and professional execution of DoD infrastructure automation (Ansible/Terraform) provide the necessary foundational competencies to successfully integrate this heterogeneous stack within the master's thesis timeframe.

# **F. References Cited** {#f.-references-cited}

\[1\] Castro, M., & Liskov, B. (1999). Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance. Proceedings of the Third Symposium on Operating Systems Design and Implementation, 173-186. 

\[2\] Das, I., Vidyarthi, D. P., & Ghosh, S. K. (2016). Multipath routing in mobile ad hoc network with probabilistic splitting of traffic. Wireless Networks, 22, 1475-1491. https://doi.org/10.1007/S11276-015-1093-Y 

\[3\] Fensterer, W., et al. (2024). Rescuing the Unreachable: Personnel Recovery and Resupply in a Contested Environment. Journal of Advanced Military Studies, 15(1), 88-105. 

\[4\] Goldwasser, S., Micali, S., & Rackoff, C. (1989). The Knowledge Complexity of Interactive Proof Systems. SIAM Journal on Computing, 18(1), 186-208. 

\[5\] Lamport, L., Shostak, R., & Pease, M. (1982). The Byzantine Generals Problem. ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, 4(3), 382-401. 

\[6\] Perkins, C. E., & Royer, E. M. (1999). Ad-hoc On-Demand Distance Vector Routing. Proceedings of the 2nd IEEE Workshop on Mobile Computing Systems and Applications, 90-100. 

\[7\] Sarr, C., & Guerin-Lassous, I. (2007). Estimating Average End-to-End Delays in IEEE 802.11 Multihop Wireless Networks. IEEE Communications Letters, 11(5), 417-419. 

\[8\] Schlichting, G. S., et al. (2024). Contested Logistics Operating Under Digital Support. AIAA SCITECH 2024 Forum. https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0481 

\[9\] Schnorr, C. P. (1991). Efficient Signature Generation by Smart Cards. Journal of Cryptology, 4(3), 161-174.
